issues.title: Practical view of computational analysis
issues.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
issues.body:
From [proposed PR](https://github.com/muhammad-usama-sardar/risks-of-mlkem/pull/5/changes#diff-4fa784baf8475c32070d6723d7a273efdfc4344ce4a2c2241d9d4089724809b6R169)

> Wrong ciphertexts, decapsulation failures, wrong transcript context, or wrong group selection must not lead to a usable traffic secret.

Is computational analysis actually implying this?

comments.author: B1ueD0g
comments.body:
I would not read the existing computational analyses as directly implying those concrete implementation negative cases.

More precise framing: the analyses identify protocol/model assumptions that implementations need to preserve at the TLS integration boundary. In particular, the KEM shared secret should be bound to the negotiated group and transcript, and traffic secrets should only be used after the normal TLS handshake checks succeed.

For ML-KEM specifically, "decapsulation failure" is imprecise because ML-KEM uses implicit rejection rather than a conventional failure signal. A bad ciphertext should result in different derived secrets and then fail at Finished; malformed key_share, wrong group, wrong transcript context, and fallback behavior are TLS integration / conformance checks around the proof assumptions, not direct conclusions of the computational proof.

Possible shorter text for later:

> For implementers, these analyses are best read as identifying assumptions the TLS implementation must preserve, not as replacing malformed-input, API-misuse, or fail-closed testing.

issues.title: Thorough review
issues.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
issues.body:
Include the technical points from the review: 

https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/tls/jlsYHENwqMv-4XPRvunqKsAL36k/

comments.author: B1ueD0g
comments.body:
Initial pass over Joshua's review against the current editor's copy.

I would filter out WG-process, government-rulemaking, and recommendation-status arguments, and keep only the technical facets below. Some are already covered; I do not think all need new text.

1. General deployment / code complexity. The draft already says cost needs analysis, but the code-complexity subpoint could be more explicit: in broad TLS deployments, ECDHE compatibility remains required for a long time, so standalone ML-KEM does not automatically reduce implementation complexity. This is different from constrained or closed deployments where endpoints are controlled.

2. Wire size / fragmentation. The cost table covers key_share bytes, but the review also asks whether ClientHello fragmentation or middlebox pressure is materially improved by removing the X25519 part. If kept, I would frame this as a measurement question, not as a claim.

3. Constrained / embedded deployments. The review asks for concrete implementer evidence on whether hybrid really blocks adoption in constrained environments, or whether standalone only helps in narrow endpoint-controlled cases. The current cost section partly covers this, but not the implementer-evidence angle.

4. Structured-lattice confidence. The current "Shiny New Crypto" section is intentionally short. One technical facet from the review is that confidence in ML-KEM depends not only on age, but also on the structured-lattice setting, reduction assumptions, and ongoing cryptanalysis. This may be a small addition there if we want that facet visible.

5. Alternative hybrid design points. If the motivation is size, constrained deployment, or compatibility, the review points toward asking whether a different hybrid point could address the concern before moving to standalone. This may already be covered by "depth of hybrids", but it could be made slightly more concrete.

6. Implementation novelty / stable-reference value. The review questions what behavior is uniquely specified by the standalone draft beyond assigned codepoints and removing ECDH from the hybrid construction. This is partly a publication-necessity point, so I would be careful. If added, I would phrase it neutrally as an implementation-maintainability question, not as a process argument.

Already covered reasonably well in the current text: formal-methods framing, implementation negative cases, cost not being one scalar value, urgency/timing, publication necessity at a high level, patents/IPR, newness/early-analysis, and the hybrid/standalone security comparison.

Potential minimal PR later: one compact paragraph each for (1) compatibility/code complexity, (2) wire-size/fragmentation as a measurement question, and (3) structured-lattice confidence. I would not touch WG-process or federal-policy arguments.

comments.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
comments.body: Thanks. Sounds good, PR is welcome on wherever existing points can be improved or where new points can be added. Please note my latest reorganization.

comments.author: B1ueD0g

issues.title: Focus on the positives and negatives
issues.author: muhammad-usama-sardar

issues.title: Expose the complexities and facets of the issue
issues.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
issues.body: Separate out primitive vs. protocol integration

comments.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
comments.body: Should we use it as a top-level distinction?

comments.author: B1ueD0g
comments.body:
I think the current top-level split into primitive-level and protocol-level is useful and probably enough for now.

My suggestion would be:

- keep primitive-level for questions about ML-KEM/X25519 assumptions, CRQC impact, structured-lattice confidence, patents, and designer recommendations;
- keep protocol-level for TLS integration, deployment cost, codepoint/publication questions, implementation bugs, hybrid depth, and formal-methods scope;
- avoid adding deployment-level or implementation-level as additional top-level sections unless those parts grow substantially.

That keeps the document readable as a primer and avoids turning the taxonomy itself into another debate. The deployment/implementation points can still appear under protocol-level, because they are mainly about how the TLS construction is integrated, deployed, and validated.

I opened #11 to fit the review-derived cost/deployment points into the current split rather than changing the taxonomy again.

pulls.title: Add implementation-facing negative cases for hybrid key exchange
pulls.author: B1ueD0g
pulls.body:
## Summary

This PR adds a short implementation-facing negative-cases subsection after the symbolic-analysis results.

The intent is to keep this contribution separate from the FATT / symbolic-analysis wording discussion and focus only on concrete failure modes implementers can check around hybrid key exchange.

## What changed

- Adds `Implementation-Facing Negative Cases` to the draft.
- Lists cases around missing or malformed hybrid components, cross-handshake assembly, fallback to standalone behavior after hybrid negotiation, premature traffic-secret derivation, and APIs/logs that obscure whether both components were accepted.
- Clarifies that these checks are not a new formal proof obligation, but a bridge from the formal binding property to implementation failure modes.

## Validation

- Checked the diff against `upstream/main`.
- Ran `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD` successfully.
- Attempted `make`; dependency installation completed, but the build failed while `kramdown-rfc` was fetching a remote resource due to a local Ruby/OpenSSL SSL connection error. The failure did not point to the added markdown text.

reviews.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
reviews.body: Thanks for the valuable contribution. I would be happy to add it with some small changes.

comments.body:
```suggestion
The formal analysis above is not an implementation test suite and does not
```

```suggestion
* **malformed or missing shares**: The negotiated group is a hybrid group, but one component of the peer
```

```suggestion
* **mixed transcript context**: The ECDHE and KEM values are individually well-formed, but assembled
```

```suggestion
* **fallback after hybrid negotiation**: A peer attempts to continue the handshake as standalone ECDHE or
```

```suggestion
* **premature secret derivation**: Application traffic secrets are derived before both hybrid components
```

```suggestion
* **API/logging ambiguity**: Exported state, logs, traces, or implementation APIs make a hybrid
```

pulls.title: Add implementation argument matrix
pulls.author: B1ueD0g
pulls.body:
This adds a short implementation-facing argument matrix after the negative-case section. The goal is to map the recurring hybrid-vs-standalone discussion to concrete review questions without touching the abstract or introduction while those sections are still being revised.

Checks:
- git diff --check
- make -n

Note: a full local make was attempted, but kramdown-rfc failed while fetching an existing remote resource over SSL before XML generation. I did not treat that as a document-content failure for this small patch.

comments.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
comments.body:
Thanks very much for your valuable contributions.
I am not an expert here. Merging it as is for -03 for WG review.

I kept the scope to the Section 5 framing after your clarification. `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD` passes locally. `make` still hits the Ruby/OpenSSL remote-resource fetch error I mentioned earlier, so I am treating that as a local setup issue rather than a markdown change issue.

pulls.title: Clean up introduction framing for ISE review
pulls.author: B1ueD0g
pulls.body:
This is a small follow-up to the ISE first-review wording cleanup, intentionally separate from PR #3.

It keeps the change limited to the abstract/objectives/introduction area:

- replaces "technical viewpoints" in the abstract with "technical facets";
- removes the short Section 1.1.2-style subsection that framed the issue as differing views;
- rewrites the early "opinions of the community" sentence as a concrete engineering question about assumptions and failure conditions.

I left Section 5 untouched here because PR #3 already handles that larger reframing.

Validation performed locally:

- `git diff --check`

comments.author: B1ueD0g
comments.body:
Addressed the two review comments and force-pushed.

I restored the short objectives subsection, but reframed it as "Presenting Different Technical Facets" so it still justifies the later facets section without using the previous viewpoints wording. I also rewrote the early ML-KEM sentence to keep the spectrum boundary explicit: ML-KEM remains secure for the deployment horizon vs. ML-KEM fails earlier than expected.

`git diff --check` passes and the GitHub editor-copy check is green.

I think the problematic part was removed already. 
I think we still need it, because the draft spends quite some space (Sec. 6) on it. How do we justify that without this? We can change the words but this point is somehow required.
This is a bit hard for me to undestand. The previous statement was covering boundaries of the spectrum, which I see missing here.

reviews.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
reviews.body: The change in formal model is not technically correct. If ML-KEM is broken, no formal analysis is required. Will commit and merge.

comments.body:
```suggestion
The opinions of the community on this matter vary from "ML-KEM is secure" to "ML-KEM is probably already secrectly broken."
```

```suggestion
```

pulls.title: Add implementation-oriented reading of formal results
pulls.author: B1ueD0g

comments.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
comments.body: Thanks a lot. Merging with some changes, and opened https://github.com/muhammad-usama-sardar/risks-of-mlkem/issues/6 for remaining point that I am not sure.
I am not sure if this last sentence is justified by these papers.

```suggestion
Instead of asking how hard ML-KEM or ECDHE is to break, the analysis may ask whether security properties still hold under Dolev-Yao attacker if one component secret is already available to the adversary.
```
I would like to keep quantum circuit point, because that is the technical detail here. 'may not be' is too vague. You can add your last sentence on top.

```suggestion
The technical details behind some public timeline claims are not yet fully public. In particular, Google has not even released the **quantum circuit** underlying their recent claims -- apparently the reason for this urgency.
These claims are therefore best treated as deployment-planning inputs rather than as proof of a specific CRQC arrival date.
```

```suggestion
```

pulls.title: Add deployment facets from review
pulls.author: B1ueD0g
pulls.body:
## Summary

Adds a small set of technical facets from the thorough review pass in #7:

- clarifies that ML-KEM confidence is not only about calendar age, but also structured-lattice assurance, parameter choices, reduction assumptions, and implementation behavior
- frames wire-size / middlebox questions as full-handshake measurement questions rather than key_share-byte claims
- separates broad Internet deployments from closed or constrained endpoint-controlled deployments when discussing implementation and operational cost
- notes that alternative hybrid design points may be relevant when the motivation is size, constrained deployment, or operational simplicity

## Validation

- `git diff --check` passes
- `make` was attempted locally, but failed in the existing local Ruby/OpenSSL remote-resource fetch path: `SSL_connect SYSCALL returned=5 ... kramdown-rfc2629 get_and_write_resource`. This appears to be the same local environment/network issue seen before, not a markdown diff error.

Related to #7.

comments.author: B1ueD0g
comments.body: I reworked the intro text again and kept the split explicit as primitive-level vs protocol-level, including a single explicit sentence to prevent formal-method claims from being mixed into deployment/operational points. Also rebased both PRs onto upstream/main@302f368 to keep context aligned. If you want, I can provide a minimal diff excerpt next.

comments.author: B1ueD0g
comments.body:
I rechecked the two review points against the current diff.

- On the low-confidence/new-crypto point: yes, the intended framing is primitive-level risk management, not a protocol-level formal-methods claim. The added sentence now says this is not a proof that standalone ML-KEM is unsafe; it is an assurance/risk input around structured-lattice assumptions, parameters, reductions, and implementation behavior.
- On ClientHello size: yes, I mean the full handshake shape, including other extensions already present in the deployment, record boundaries, fragmentation behavior, and retry paths. The point is that key_share bytes alone do not settle the middlebox/interoperability question.

So my reading is that the primitive/protocol split is now explicit enough. If you prefer, I can still drop either paragraph rather than keep iterating on wording.

reviews.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
reviews.body: Please recheck that the added comments are still aligned with primitive-level and protocol-level division.

comments.body: Try to defocus proofs. Do I understand correctly that you are supporting 'new crypto' point by low confidence points.
By ClientHello size, you mean dependency on other extensions, right?

pulls.title: Defocus formal methods in framing
pulls.author: B1ueD0g
pulls.body:
## Summary

Defocuses formal methods in the opening framing without changing the technical conclusions:

- makes the abstract start with technical facets and deployment reasoning rather than formal analysis
- describes formal-analysis work as one input to the broader discussion
- renames the introductory subsection to "Formal Methods as One Input"
- adjusts the motivation text to foreground primitive assumptions, protocol integration, implementation behavior, and operational risk

This is intended to address the framing direction in #10 while preserving the existing formal-methods content.

## Validation

- `git diff --check` passes
- `make` was attempted locally, but failed in the existing macOS Ruby/OpenSSL remote-resource fetch path: `SSL_connect SYSCALL returned=5 ... kramdown-rfc2629 get_and_write_resource`. This is the same local environment/network failure seen on the previous PRs, not a markdown diff failure.

Addresses #10.

comments.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
comments.body: Sorry, I was simultaneously working on it. I've pushed my local changes. Please rebase on my changes.

comments.author: B1ueD0g
comments.body: Done — I rebased this PR onto upstream/main@302f368 and kept the scope changes minimal. The intent is still the same: framing formal methods as one input, not a substitute for deployment/integration text.

comments.author: B1ueD0g
comments.body: Fixed one small structure issue after rechecking the rendered section: the branch had kept the old `### Technical Insights` heading directly before `### Formal Methods as One Input`. I removed the old heading so the subsection now has a single title. `git diff --check` passes.

pulls.title: Clarify implementer reading of computational analyses
pulls.author: B1ueD0g
pulls.body:
This is a small follow-up to the discussion in #6.

The change keeps the computational-analysis section useful for implementers while narrowing the claim:

- the analyses are read as key-schedule and transcript-binding checks;
- they do not replace malformed-input, API-misuse, or fallback testing;
- for ML-KEM, invalid ciphertext handling is framed around implicit rejection and TLS-level outcomes, not a conventional decapsulation-failure signal;
- the implementation-facing question is whether inconsistent KEM inputs, wrong group selection, transcript mismatch, or incomplete fallback paths can produce a usable handshake or silently continue under a different negotiated security property.

I kept this separate from #11 and #12 so it can be reviewed independently and skipped if it is too late for -04.

Validation:

- `git diff --check` passes.
- Local `make` did not complete because kramdown failed while fetching an external resource over Ruby/OpenSSL (`SSL_connect SYSCALL returned=5`), before producing XML. I did not see a Markdown/diff-check issue locally; the GitHub editor-copy check should be the useful build signal here.

Related to #6.
